headline: The Skeeters Are Built Different, The Bombers Are Peaking, and Someone in Canberra Just Dropped Roki Sasaki
subheadline: Week 5 gave us a 52-run offensive explosion, a record-tying strikeout performance, and at least one roster decision that will haunt someone's dreams for weeks.
body:
## Thriller of the Week

### General Hospital vs. Hiroshima Radiation — 5-5

This one had everything. **General Hospital** rolled out what might be the most aesthetically pleasing pitching staff in the league — Wrobleski with a 0.69 ERA, Fried with a 0.63 WHIP, five quality starts — and still couldn't pull away. Why? Because **Hiroshima Radiation** is running what amounts to a closer factory over there, dropping 12 NSVH on the scoreboard like it's nothing, and McLean went absolutely nuclear with 17 strikeouts that would've been the story of any other week. This is the fantasy equivalent of watching a Spurs-era Tim Duncan team get locked in a 95-94 overtime game against a team that just shoots threes and sells out for stops — two completely different philosophies, neither blinking.

The tie stings for both squads for different reasons. **General Hospital** brought its Sunday best and walked away with a single point. **Hiroshima** had the saves-and-holds category on lockdown but couldn't manufacture enough offense to flip the script. With both teams now sitting in a standings logjam in the middle of the pack (which, in a 14-team league, is starting to feel like one of those Season 3 *Survivor* episodes where nobody knows who to trust), Week 6 suddenly carries enormous weight for both rosters.

---

## The Week's Defining Moment

Let's just say it plainly: the **Sugar Land Skeeters** scored 52 runs this week. Fifty-two. The league average is somewhere in the mid-to-high 20s. The Skeeters nearly *doubled* it. This is the "what if the Showtime Lakers had two Shaqs" version of a fantasy week — the kind of output where you stop trying to explain it and just start pointing. Fifteen home runs. Thirty-eight RBI. Eight players reaching at least three runs scored. Against a **North Correa** squad that brought legitimate pitching (Glasnow posted a 0.25 WHIP, Sale came in at 0.50), the Skeeters somehow looked like they were playing a Little League team from a different zip code.

What makes this particularly alarming for the rest of the league is that this wasn't a fluke-pile-up where every roll of the dice went hot. The Skeeters have been top-two in category wins every single week of this season. This isn't a team getting lucky in one blowout week — this is a roster that is simply constructed better than almost everyone else, hitting on all cylinders, and refusing to give anyone a category for free. They led the league in runs (52), finished second in RBI (38), and put up seven NSVH for second in that category too. The only real vulnerability in their profile? One quality start and a 4.75 ERA that suggests the rotation is the one area where challengers could theoretically attack.

Meanwhile, on the other side of the ledger, the **Albuquerque Bombers** delivered the most lopsided *result* of the week — an 8-1 demolition of the **Wisconsin Rapids Reapers** that felt like the Bombers were playing on a different difficulty setting. Kyle Harrison and Landen Roupp were simply unhittable (more on that in the record-breaking section below), and the lineup additions of Jung and Lile gave this team immediate real-world production. The Bombers led the league in ERA (3.05), K (77), and QS (5) this week. Combined with the Skeeters' offensive dominance, Week 5 made one thing crystal clear: the gap between the league's elite and the teams still searching for an identity is real, it's growing, and the middle of the pack is running out of weeks to figure it out.

---

## Matchup Deep-Dives

### Sugar Land Skeeters def. North Correa, 5-4

Already covered the run-scoring apocalypse above, but let me add some context to the **North Correa** side of this that deserves acknowledgment. Getting two elite pitching performances (Glasnow and Sale, both with sub-0.50 WHIPs) and still losing 5-4 is the fantasy equivalent of going 4-for-4 with a walk and losing on a walkoff — it's not your fault and it's completely your fault at the same time. Ten stolen bases kept North Correa dangerous in the speed categories, and Ohtani's multi-category presence continues to make this roster feel like it's always *one good week* away from something. The addition of Austin Wells at catcher (more below) is smart housekeeping. But they need the offense to show up alongside those elite arms, because right now the pitching is writing checks the lineup can't always cash.

### Albuquerque Bombers def. Wisconsin Rapids Reapers, 8-1

This was a paddling. The **Bombers** won eight of twelve scoring categories and it honestly could've been worse. Harrison and Roupp combined for 28 strikeouts on their own — the rest of the staff filled in around them to hit 77 total K on the week, tying a league record (more below). The **Reapers**, for their part, posted the league's best OBP at .374 — which is a weird flex to have in a week where you got beaten in basically every pitching category and finished with a 5.81 ERA. Dropping McGreevy without a clear replacement incoming is exactly the kind of front-office move that signals a team trying to rearrange deck chairs rather than fix the engine. The Reapers are trending the wrong direction and Week 6 isn't going to get easier.

### Riverside Roughnecks def. Athletic Park Dragons, 6-4

This is the sneaky best result of the week and nobody's talking about it enough. The **Roughnecks** beat a team that put up 13 home runs and 39 RBI — the most RBI in the league this week. Let that sink in. Kurtz went for 8 runs and 3 home runs. Suzuki added 3 home runs. The Roughnecks put up 12 HR and 36 runs themselves and then buried the **Dragons** with five quality starts and a rotation that is quietly becoming a real asset. Meanwhile, the Dragons are doing the fantasy equivalent of scoring touchdowns in a basketball game — the offensive ceiling is elite, but their pitching posted a 6.31 ERA with *zero quality starts* in 35.2 innings. Zero. That's not a slump, that's a structural problem, and until they fix it, they're going to keep losing matchups they have no business losing.

### Abbotsford-Colby Twins def. BaseballPro77, 5-3

**BaseballPro77** outscored their opponent in runs (36 to 31) and outhit them, and still lost. That's the category-scoring format doing its thing. The **Twins** threw 83.2 innings — the most in the league this week — and ground it out across the pitching categories while Aranda's power and Griffin's performance anchored the highlights. **BaseballPro77** isn't broken by any means — Murakami, Trout, and Basallo's combined .611 OBP is the kind of on-base production most teams would auction a draft pick for — but the pitching has been inconsistent enough to cost them multiple winnable matchups. Strahm's addition (from Wonderdogs, more on that below) should help shore up the bullpen, but this team needs to find pitching consistency to stop bleeding points in close matchups.

### Buttville Skidmarks def. Benchwarmers, 6-4

The **Benchwarmers** ran the league-leading 11 stolen bases this week and still only won four categories. That's a microcosm of this entire roster: genuinely elite in one area, suspect in almost everything else. Three home runs. A 5.28 ERA. And they just dropped Scherzer and Assad from the rotation without meaningful replacements coming in. The **Skidmarks** weren't exactly dominant here — 24 runs scored is near the bottom of the league, and Bradish's WHIP was an adventure — but De La Cruz and Caminero provided real power, and five NSVH is a category weapon that won this matchup in the end. This was less "Buttville wins" and more "Benchwarmers handed it to them."

### Canberra Wombats 5, Wonder Wharf Wonderdogs 5 — TIE

See the Lucky/Unlucky section immediately below, because this matchup basically wrote it for me.

---

## Lucky/Unlucky Team of the Week

**Lucky Team: Benchwarmers.** The +1.33 luck delta is the highest in the league, and this 6-4 result against the Skidmarks is Exhibit A in the case for "variance as a roster strategy." Three home runs. A 5.28 ERA. No real power profile to speak of. And they somehow scraped out a win primarily on the strength of stolen bases. I want to be clear: the Benchwarmers are playing with house money right now. You cannot sustain a +1.33 luck delta for a full season. At some point, the variance gods will come to collect, and when they do, this roster — which just dropped Scherzer and Assad from the rotation — is going to need real contributors to absorb the blow.

**Unlucky Team: Wonder Wharf Wonderdogs.** The -0.62 luck delta is the worst in the league and it is becoming a personality trait for this team. This week, Harris delivered nine RBI. Moniak hit two home runs. The pitching staff posted a 4.06 ERA. They threw 68.2 innings — second most in the league. And the best they could do was a 5-5 tie with the Wombats. At some point this roster has to catch a break, right? The Dollander add gives them legitimate rotation upside heading into Week 6, but the losses (and ties) are piling up in the standings while this team keeps doing everything reasonably right and getting almost nothing back for it. This is the *No Country for Old Men* of fantasy seasons — doing everything correctly and still watching Anton Chigurh walk away.

---

## League Record Alert

The **Albuquerque Bombers** tied the single-week strikeout record with 77 K, matching the mark the **Abbotsford-Colby Twins** set back in Week 2. Kyle Harrison and Landen Roupp combined for 28 strikeouts alone — against a Reapers team that, to be fair, was playing behind a porous offensive approach. The record-tying performance punctuates what was the most dominant pitching week any staff has put together since that Week 2 Twin performance, and it raises a real question heading into the second half of the season: is this Bombers rotation the best in the league? After this week, it's genuinely hard to argue otherwise.

---

## Manager Spotlight: Albuquerque Bombers

Let's talk about the best-managed roster in the league right now, because the **Albuquerque Bombers** are doing everything right and it's worth acknowledging.

This team is sitting at #3 in the power rankings after an 8-1 demolition of the Reapers, and the move chart this week reads like a fantasy baseball masterclass. Four waiver adds — Jung at third base, Garrett and Winn for rotation depth, Marte for lineup flexibility — all while dropping two roster cloggers who weren't contributing. Jung immediately chipped in a home run, three runs, three RBI, and a stolen base in his debut week. That's not luck. That's a manager who knows exactly what gaps need filling and fills them before the week starts.

The pitching staff is operating at a level that should genuinely scare the teams above them in the standings. Kyle Harrison and Landen Roupp headlining a rotation that led the league in ERA (3.05), K (77), and QS (5) is the kind of pitching profile that wins championships in this format. The offense is coming along — 28 runs, 10 home runs, 8 stolen bases isn't a liability — and with Jung now stabilizing the lineup and more depth added, the Bombers look like the most complete roster in the league from a *construction* standpoint, even if the Skeeters still have the raw firepower advantage.

Playoff outlook? If they keep the waiver wire this active and Harrison stays healthy, the Bombers are a legitimate title contender. This manager is locked in.

---

## Trade Value Watch

**Kyle Harrison, SP — Albuquerque Bombers.** After anchoring the Bombers' record-tying 77-strikeout week with a 1.00 ERA and 15 strikeouts, Harrison is arguably the hottest pitcher in the league right now. **HOLD.** You don't sell peak value in a championship window — and the Bombers are clearly in one. If you own Harrison, enjoy it. If you want him, you're going to overpay significantly, and you should probably do it anyway.

**Roki Sasaki, SP — Now on someone's waiver wire, apparently.** The **Canberra Wombats** dropped Sasaki this week in favor of JR Ritchie, which is a decision so baffling I genuinely thought it was a typo when I first read it. **BUY (if available).** If Sasaki is somehow sitting on your league's waiver wire, you drop whatever you have to drop and you add him immediately. This is a high-upside arm with a ceiling that dwarfs anything named Ritchie in any context.

**Elly De La Cruz, — Buttville Skidmarks.** De La Cruz continues to flash as a genuine multi-category contributor — power, speed, the kind of player who can single-handedly win you a week in two or three categories. **HOLD/BUY.** If the Skidmarks are willing to deal given their inconsistent scoring offense, there might be a buy-low window here. De La Cruz is exactly the kind of player whose value in a category league is probably *underpriced* given how hard it is to find that skill set on the wire.

---

## Power Rankings

1. **Sugar Land Skeeters** | 52 runs and 15 HR remain the most jaw-dropping offensive week of the season; this lineup has no clear weakness and the Skeeters have been top-two in category wins every single week [SAME]
2. **General Hospital** | A frustrating 5-5 tie despite Wrobleski (0.69 ERA) and Fried (0.63 WHIP) carrying the rotation; Kumar Rocker now added to an already elite staff makes this team the most frightening on paper [SAME]
3. **Albuquerque Bombers** | The 8-1 demolition of the Reapers was the statement win of the week — Harrison and Roupp were unhittable, Jung and Lile added immediate lineup value, and four waiver adds show a manager locked in and building [UP]
4. **Hiroshima Radiation** | McLean's 17 K and 12 team NSVH are legitimate category weapons; a 5-5 tie that felt winnable stings, but the saves-and-holds moat is real and this roster is deeper than its record suggests [UP]
5. **BaseballPro77** | Lost 5-3 but Murakami, Trout, and Basallo's .611 OBP all showed up again; the offense is consistently elite and Strahm's addition tightens the bullpen enough to turn close losses into wins [DOWN]
6. **Athletic Park Dragons** | 13 HR and 39 RBI and still lost 6-4 because the rotation posted a 6.31 ERA with zero quality starts — the offensive ceiling is among the league's best but the pitching is a five-alarm fire [DOWN]
7. **North Correa** | Glasnow posted a 0.25 WHIP and Sale a 0.50 WHIP and they still lost 5-4 — that's a brutal pill to swallow; Ohtani's elite multi-category output and 10 team steals keep this roster dangerous week to week [SAME]
8. **Riverside Roughnecks** | Quietly one of the best stories of the week: beat a loaded Dragons squad with Kurtz (8 R, 3 HR), Suzuki (3 HR), and 5 QS from a rotation that is starting to look like a real strength [UP]
9. **Abbotsford-Colby Twins** | Aranda's 3 HR and Griffin's dominant outing were encouraging, but a -0.43 luck delta and a reliance on a thin pitching staff means the 5-3 win may flatter this roster a touch [SAME]
10. **Canberra Wombats** | Raleigh (4 HR) and Walker (3 HR) kept this team alive for a 5-5 tie but Nola's 11.00 ERA and a team ERA of 5.59 with just 2 QS underscore how fragile this rotation has become after dropping Sasaki [DOWN]
11. **Buttville Skidmarks** | De La Cruz and Caminero are a real power tandem and 5 NSVH is a genuine asset, but 24 runs scored and Bradish's 2.03 WHIP leave this roster feeling like it needs another consistent bat to push upward [SAME]
12. **Wisconsin Rapids Reapers** | Got blown out 8-1 across nearly every pitching category, and dropping McGreevy without a clear replacement signals a front office without answers; this roster is trending the wrong direction [DOWN]
13. **Wonder Wharf Wonderdogs** | The -0.62 luck delta is now a recurring identity crisis — Harris, Moniak, and solid pitching all week produced only a tie; Dollander's addition offers rotation hope but the losses are piling up in the standings [SAME]
14. **Benchwarmers** | A +1.33 luck delta is the most unsustainable number in the league; 3 HR, a 5.28 ERA, and dropping Scherzer and Assad leave this roster with very little margin for error going forward [SAME]

---

## Waiver Wire

**[A] Albuquerque Bombers** — Added Jung (3B), Garrett (SP), Winn (SP,RP), Marte (3B,OF); dropped Antonacci, Loáisiga — Jung immediately contributed 3 R, 1 HR, 3 RBI, and 1 SB in his debut week while Garrett and Winn add rotation depth to what is already the hottest pitching staff in the league. This is the most aggressive and effective waiver week of the season. The Bombers manager is operating at a different level right now.

**[A] General Hospital** — Added Kumar Rocker (SP); dropped Leiter, Junk — One move, maximum impact. Rocker slides into an already elite rotation featuring Wrobleski and Fried, making **General Hospital**'s pitching staff arguably the scariest in the league. If you're a middle-of-the-pack team looking up at this rotation, Week 6 scheduling against these guys just got considerably more stressful.

**[B] Wonder Wharf Wonderdogs** — Added Dollander (SP,RP), Vargas (1B,2B), Bruihl (RP), Miller (RP); dropped Flaherty, Strahm, Sterner, Clarke — Losing Strahm hurts — he immediately showed up on BaseballPro77's roster — but Dollander is a genuine high-upside add that this team desperately needed. The volume of moves raises an eyebrow. Is this a plan or is this panic? We'll know by Week 7.

**[C] Benchwarmers** — Added Schmitt (1B,2B,3B); dropped Naylor (1B), Scherzer (SP), Assad (SP) — Dropping two starting pitchers when you're already sitting on a 5.28 ERA is the kind of move that makes you put your phone face-down on the table. Schmitt adds positional flexibility but doesn't move the needle on power or pitching, the two areas this roster most needs help.

**[B] North Correa** — Added Wells (C); dropped Realmuto (C) — Smart, clean, no-drama upgrade. Realmuto is in decline, Wells is ascending, and **North Correa** quietly maintained positional depth at catcher without surrendering anything valuable. This is exactly how you manage a contending roster.

**[B] Riverside Roughnecks** — Added Bachman (RP), Vodnik (RP); dropped Vientos (1B,3B) — Losing Vientos's bat from the lineup is a real cost, but the **Roughnecks** identified their actual problem (bullpen depth killing QS value) and addressed it directly. For a team coming off a 6-4 upset win, this is a manager building toward sustainability rather than one-week pops.

**[D] Canberra Wombats** — Added Ritchie (SP); dropped Sasaki (SP) — I've been doing this long enough to know that sometimes there are injury concerns or real-world roster situations that explain moves like this. But from the outside, dropping Roki Sasaki — a pitcher with genuine elite upside — for JR Ritchie, in a week where your team ERA is already 5.59 and Nola is posting an 11.00 ERA? This is the Canberra front office handing their opponents a gift. If this decision isn't reversed quickly, Week 6 could turn into a genuine pitching catastrophe.
